title: The drivers of foreign currency‐based banking in Central and Eastern Europe
abstract: Abstract Foreign currency (FX)‐based loans and deposits became very popular in Central and Eastern European countries (CEECs) over the 2000–2011 period. In this paper, I simultaneously examine the demand‐side (consumer‐related) and supply‐side (bank‐related) determinants of the quick spread of FX banking. I use a newly constructed dataset on FX and domestic currency loans, deposits and interest rates, covering 16 CEECs overtime. Local‐FX interest rate and market share spreads are: (1) lower in managed currency regimes; (2) strongly affected by the prevalence of FX funding, currency mismatch and FX banking restrictions, and (3) wider after economic crises.
summary_en: Why did foreign-currency banking spread so quickly across Central and Eastern Europe in 2000-2011, and what can macroprudential policy learn from it? Temesváry uses a new dataset on FX and domestic-currency loans, deposits, and interest rates across 16 CEE countries. Both demand-side (consumer) and supply-side (bank) determinants matter. Three findings: (1) local-FX interest-rate and market-share spreads are lower in managed currency regimes, (2) they are strongly affected by FX-funding prevalence, currency mismatch, and FX banking restrictions, (3) they widen after economic crises. The paper provides the cleanest decomposition to date of the FX-banking phenomenon that became Hungary's biggest macroprudential problem.
data_used: Newly constructed dataset on FX and domestic-currency loans, deposits, and interest rates for 16 CEE countries, 2000-2011. Country-level panel regressions with currency-regime, capital-flow, and crisis controls.
policy_relevance: Direct relevance to MNB macroprudential policy and to Hungarian retrospective analysis of the 2008-2014 FX-mortgage crisis. The paper's finding that FX-banking restrictions matter supports the post-2014 Hungarian regulatory framework that explicitly limited FX household lending. Useful baseline for evaluating CEE-wide macroprudential coordination via the European Systemic Risk Board.
